data.title: Heritage Financial projects noninterest expense of $56M-$57M in Q4 as Olympic integration continues
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Heritage Financial (HFWA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We closed the merger with Olympic Bancorp during the first quarter, better positioning our company for growth in the Puget Sound market." (CEO, President & Director Bryan McDonald)
  • "We are converting systems in late September and we will be carrying higher expenses until after the conversion." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "We expect the upward trajectory to continue, primarily driven by new loans and repricing within the existing loan portfolio." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "Total loan balances increased $939 million in the first quarter" and "Total deposits increased $1.33 billion in Q1." (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "The net interest margin increased to 3.96% from 3.72% in the prior quarter" and "We recognized a reversal of provision for credit losses in the amount of $1.03 million in Q1." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "We are expecting quarterly noninterest expense levels to increase to an average of approximately $64 million to $65 million in Q2 and Q3 before decreasing to a range of $56 million to $57 million in Q4." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "Nonaccrual loans totaled $15 million at quarter end" and "there were no nonaccrual loans in the acquired Olympic portfolio at quarter end." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Tony Chalfant)
  • "Our ratio of total nonowner-occupied CRE loans to total loans moved just above the regulatory guidance level to 301%." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant)
  • "For the quarter, our commercial team closed $166 million in new loan commitments" and "Based on the current pipeline, we expect an annualized loan growth rate in the mid-single-digit range in the next couple of quarters." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)

Outlook

  • "Based on the current pipeline, we expect an annualized loan growth rate in the mid-single-digit range in the next couple of quarters." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "Yes. I think we're going to continue to see margin expansion, not going to be significant" and "I expect to reach the 4% by the end of the year or before." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "I think we're going to stay right around that for the remainder of the year, hovering around 1.70%." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s discussion of integration timing, management reiterated the conversion timing as "late September" and maintained that expenses stay elevated until after conversion. (CEO, President & Director McDonald; Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Financial results

  • "Q1 yields in the loan portfolio were 5.73%" and "The cost of interest-bearing deposits decreased to 1.71% from 1.83% in the prior quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "The net interest margin increased to 3.96% from 3.72% in the prior quarter and from 3.44% in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "We recognized a reversal of provision for credit losses in the amount of $1.03 million in Q1" and management tied it to "adjusting the allowance from 1.10% at the end of 2025 to 1.06% at the end of Q1." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "The increase in the noninterest expense was also due to merger-related costs of $5.2 million" and "intangible asset amortization expense of $2.1 million" (each compared by management to $385,000 and $285,000, respectively, in the prior quarter). (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "Our TCE ratio was 9.6% at the end of Q1 compared to 10.1% in the prior quarter" and "The decrease in the TCE ratio was expected due to the impact of the merger." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Expenses looked "kind of high" and asked whether the $64 million to $65 million outlook includes merger costs; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "yes -- yes, that does include the merger-related expenses" and "If you take out merger costs, we're more in the $57 million to $58 million range" with "dropping to about $55 million by Q4."
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked where the $5 million-plus merger costs sit by line item; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "professional services would be a big one" plus "compensation because of severance" and "some contract stuff" that could show in "data processing," adding "I don't have it broken out by type."
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about margin impacts and March run-rate; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "On the interest reversals, it was 5" (basis points) to margin, and "if you take out the interest reversals for March NIM, it was 3.95%."
  • Jackson Laurent, Stephens: Asked what changed loan-growth expectations and early read on Kitsap bankers; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "The driver behind the go-forward growth rate is really the change in the pipeline" and "we feel good about that pipeline driving kind of mid-single-digit loan growth"; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant: "the credit cultures were pretty similar" and "we're feeling pretty comfortable."
  • Laurent, Stephens: Pressed on deposit repricing risk; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "We did raise our very highest rate" and "I really don't expect it to move a whole lot"; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "we are seeing strong deposit competition" while citing "good success with our relationship strategy."
  • Laurent, Stephens: Asked about buybacks/loss trades; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "We don't foresee at this point any loss trades" and "We still have about 800,000 shares left in our current repurchase plan" and "we may be active this quarter."
  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: Asked about recruiting amid "disruption" and whether hiring is embedded in expense run-rate; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "We are out recruiting" but "we're not seeing necessarily an increase in total banker headcount" and hiring has been "onesie, twosie" with retirements.
  • Driscoll, KBW: Asked whether integration is ahead/behind schedule and retention/synergies; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "we're right on track" and "there hasn't been any kind of negative customer response" so far, adding "we'll learn more... when we actually go through the systems conversion."
  • Evan Kwiatkowski, Raymond James: Asked about borrower sentiment/payoffs amid macro uncertainty; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "we're not seeing" customers delaying yet, and "that payoff activity is lower than what we encountered last year" with the trend expected "something lower than last year."
  • Kwiatkowski, Raymond James: Asked where pressures are building in credit; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant: "we have seen a little more pressure in the C&I portfolio" tied to "uncertainty in the economy, whether it's tariff issues, higher labor costs, supply chain issues."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on cost control and deposit competition, with questions like "it seems kind of high" on expenses (Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson) and whether competition could drive "upward migration" in deposit costs (Jackson Laurent, Stephens).
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone, emphasizing integration progress and stability, including "we're right on track" (CEO, President & Director McDonald) and "credit quality remained strong and stable" (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant), while also acknowledging uncertainty with "we are aware of the emerging risks in the economy" (Chief Credit Officer Chalfant).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s tone, management shifted from anticipating the Olympic closing to operating as a combined company and discussing concrete post-close items such as "higher expenses until after the conversion" and explicit quarterly expense ranges. (CEO, President & Director McDonald; Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The major narrative change was execution: Q4 framed Olympic as "pending" with a plan to "close at the end of January" (CEO, President & Director McDonald), while Q1 reported "We closed the merger with Olympic Bancorp during the first quarter" and outlined the "late September" conversion timeline and associated expense drag. (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • Guidance language around profitability leaned more specific in Q1, with Executive VP & CFO Hinson setting quarterly noninterest expense ranges and saying "I expect to reach the 4% by the end of the year or before" on net interest margin, compared with Q4’s broader view that margin improvement would "continue over the next year or 2" and could "potentially get near that 4% range by the end of the year." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on margin and expenses (Jeff Rulis in both quarters), but Q1 added more integration-execution questions (customer/producer retention, synergy timing) and recruiting opportunity questions tied to regional disruption. (Charles Driscoll, KBW)

Risks and concerns

  • "We are converting systems in late September and we will be carrying higher expenses until after the conversion." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "As of quarter end, our ratio of total nonowner-occupied CRE loans to total loans moved just above the regulatory guidance level to 301%" and management said, "we anticipate the ratio will decline to historical levels over time." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant)
  • "We are aware of the emerging risks in the economy and the potential impact on our credit quality" and cited pressure factors including "tariff issues, higher labor costs, supply chain issues." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant)
  • Management acknowledged market funding pressure with "strong deposit competition" while emphasizing a relationship strategy to manage mix and costs. (CEO, President & Director McDonald)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as the first quarter operating with Olympic onboard, with emphasis on a late-September systems conversion that keeps expenses elevated into Q3, an expectation that quarterly noninterest expense moderates in Q4, and a continued upward net interest margin trajectory supported by asset repricing and Olympic’s balance sheet. Credit commentary centered on stable-to-improving nonaccrual metrics, heightened attention to emerging C&I pressures tied to macro uncertainty, and monitoring a nonowner-occupied CRE concentration that moved just above regulatory guidance due to the acquisition and fair value marks.

data.title: Patterson-UTI expects to exit Q2 around 92 to 95 rigs as activity inflects higher
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I'm going to begin by saying we're hiring." (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)
  • "The commodity outlook has shifted materially since the start of the year due to heightened geopolitical risk and oil supply disruptions in the Middle East, which will likely reshape global oil supply and demand balances for several years." (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)
  • "We expect our rig count will exit the second quarter above the quarterly average and near the high point so far for the year, around 92 to 95 rigs depending on the timing." (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)
  • "Demand for Completion Services is improving, particularly in the back half of 2026. And we are in discussions with customers on higher pricing to more appropriately reflect rising demand and the high industry utilization." (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)
  • "While our Completions business has nearly 250,000 cold stacked horsepower that could technically be reactivated... Reactivating a single fleet would require more than $10 million investment... we are not prioritizing investment in these older assets." (President, CEO & Director Hendricks)
  • "Total reported revenue for the quarter was $1.117 billion... net loss attributable to common shareholders of $25 million or $0.06 per share... Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter totaled $205 million." (Executive VP & CFO C. Smith)
  • "We ended the first quarter with $337 million of cash on hand and nothing drawn on our $500 million revolving credit facility." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter in Drilling Services, we expect our rig count to average around 90 rigs... We expect adjusted gross profit in the Drilling Services segment to be approximately $130 million." (Executive VP & CFO C. Smith)
  • "For the second quarter... we expect Completion Services adjusted gross profit to be approximately $105 million, with near full utilization of our active assets." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "For the second quarter, we expect Drilling Products adjusted gross profit to decline slightly driven by lower profitability in our international business, particularly in the Middle East and the normal impact of spring breakup in Canada." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "We have much more clarity on the market direction and these dynamics point to a more constructive environment for activity and profitability." (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)

Financial Results

  • "Total reported revenue for the quarter was $1.117 billion." (Executive VP & CFO C. Smith)
  • "We reported a net loss attributable to common shareholders of $25 million or $0.06 per share." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter totaled $205 million, which included $3 million in early contract termination revenue in the Drilling Services segment." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "In our Drilling Services segment, first quarter revenue was $352 million and adjusted gross profit was $134 million." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "In our Completion Services segment, first quarter revenue was $680 million and adjusted gross profit was $98 million." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "First quarter Drilling Products revenue was $80 million and adjusted gross profit was $33 million." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "General and administrative expenses in the first quarter were $69 million." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "During the first quarter, total CapEx was $117 million." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)
  • "Our Board has approved a quarterly dividend of $0.10 per share payable June 15 to shareholders of record as of June 1." (Executive VP & CFO Smith)

Q&A

  • Saurabh Pant, BofA Securities: Asked when publics add activity and how tight capacity gets; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "we're sold out of our top-tier equipment... we're going to work on improving pricing, improving returns before we start adding capacity."
  • Saurabh Pant, BofA Securities: Asked how fast pricing resets in rigs/frac; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "we are seeing leading-edge pricing on drilling rigs starting to move up" and "some of the customers have already given us 10% price increases" on frac.
  • Derek Podhaizer, Piper Sandler: Asked rig reactivation costs and upgrade economics; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "we've got some rigs that are moving" and "$5 million in CapEx" to get rigs back, while upgraded APEX-XC day rates were described as "pushing $40,000 a day" with expectations to "exceed $40,000 a day" later.
  • Derek Podhaizer, Piper Sandler: Asked frac calendar white space and second-half visibility; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "as of 2 days ago, we've basically filled the majority of that white space" and "we feel like we're fully loaded in the third quarter."
  • James Rollyson, Raymond James: Asked completions pricing/margin path and incremental CapEx; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "we need to get the pricing back in line"; Executive VP & CFO Smith: "we're looking at places where we think there could be opportunity."
  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: Asked pricing needed for new builds and tech-tier spreads; Executive VP & CFO Smith: "you probably still need 5% to 10% additional" pricing to justify new builds, and "you may see the spread... widened" between equipment tiers.
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked where rig demand is improving and what triggers growth capital; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "we're seeing it across multiple basins" and "we've got to evaluate what that looks like from a return standpoint."
  • Keith MacKey, RBC: Asked termination revenue alongside reactivations and inflation; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "we've had termination payments" and later discussed inflation inputs including "tungsten prices"; Executive VP & CFO Smith clarified "$5 million... That's OpEx. That's not CapEx."
  • Doug Becker, Capital One: Asked how many rigs tied to $5 million and storm EBITDA impact; Executive VP & CFO Smith: "$5 million ties to that 92 to 95 exit rate"; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "It was about $9 million" storm impact.
  • Edward Kim, Barclays: Asked why rig count data lagged oil price rise and whether 105 rigs is achievable; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "these large E&Ps will relook at their budgets for 2027" and "it's too early to tell" on year-end rig count.
  • Daniel Kutz, Morgan Stanley: Asked international upside and DUC implications; President, CEO & Director Hendricks: "We did ship 2 drilling rigs down to Argentina" and on DUCs, "there could be a period that we are increasing DUCs... until we do get more completion work out there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but pressing on cycle timing and pricing mechanics, with repeated capacity questions such as "we are pretty much sold out on active equipment" (Saurabh Pant, BofA Securities) and detailed pricing-tier probing from Scott Gruber.
  • Management tone was positive and increasingly confident on activity and pricing, including "Momentum appears to be shifting back toward U.S. land activity" (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks) and "we are seeing a clear change in market tone" (President, CEO & Director Hendricks).
  • Versus the prior call’s emphasis on stability at lower prices ("With oil prices trading near $60 per barrel, my expectation is that activity in oil basins remains relatively steady" (President, CEO & Director Hendricks, Q4 2025)), the current call used more directional language around an inflection ("we expect this to drive an increase in U.S. shale activity starting later in the second quarter" (President, CEO & Director Hendricks)).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management shifted from a cautious, steady-activity framing in Q4 2025 ("there may be a moderate decrease in U.S. oil activity in the near term" (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)) to a more constructive setup tied to higher prices and reactivations in Q1 2026 ("we are increasingly hearing that the strip is likely to incentivize additional incremental oil-directed drilling and completion activity in the second half of this year" (President, CEO & Director Hendricks)).
  • The prior quarter stressed capital restraint ("we have reduced our gross CapEx budget by around 15% to roughly $500 million in 2026" (President, CEO & Director Hendricks, Q4 2025)), while the current quarter highlighted potential opportunities to lean in ("we're looking at places where we think there could be opportunity" (Executive VP & CFO C. Smith)).
  • Analyst focus tightened around pricing reset speed and capacity scarcity in Q1 2026, versus Q4 2025’s heavier emphasis on frac market measurement issues and cost structure.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The conflict in the Middle East has increased risk in one of our key regions, which contributes roughly 10% to 15% of segment revenue, primarily from Saudi Arabia." (President, CEO & Director William Hendricks)
  • "We've experienced meaningful inflation in several key inputs, particularly the material tungsten." (President, CEO & Director Hendricks)
  • "Seasonal working capital headwinds impacted free cash flow in the first quarter." (Executive VP & CFO C. Smith)
  • "Reactivating a single fleet would require more than $10 million investment" for older diesel frac equipment, and management said it was "not prioritizing investment in these older assets." (President, CEO & Director Hendricks)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an early-stage turn toward higher U.S. land activity, highlighting a plan to exit Q2 around 92 to 95 rigs, near-full utilization in completions, and active negotiations for higher pricing before adding capacity. The call also emphasized disciplined capital deployment, including reluctance to reactivate older diesel frac fleets despite tight industry capacity, while maintaining shareholder returns through a $0.10 quarterly dividend and pointing to Middle East-related risk and input inflation (notably tungsten) as key operational headwinds.

data.title: World Kinect forecasts 2026 adjusted EPS of $2.65-$2.85 as it rebrands to World Fuel
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: World Kinect (WKC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Despite a far more volatile and unpredictable environment than anyone could have expected, we delivered a strong start to 2026, driven by strong execution and the continued benefits of our focused portfolio strategy.” (CEO & Director Ira Birns)

  • “We announced today that World Fuel will serve as our unified corporate and commercial brand for substantially all internal and external purposes… World Kinect will remain as our corporate legal name and our ticker symbol will remain as WKC.” (CEO & Director Birns)

  • “Marine results were consistent with what we have long communicated, when prices rise materially and volatility increases, this business performs exceptionally well.” (CEO & Director Birns)

  • “Aviation also exceeded expectations this quarter… The integration of the Universal Trip Support Services business is well underway, and we are pleased with both its performance and how effectively the teams are coming together.” (CEO & Director Birns)

  • “Total non-GAAP adjustments in the first quarter were approximately $16 million or $13 million after tax.” (Executive VP & CFO Jose-Miguel Tejada)

  • “On a consolidated basis, first quarter volume was 4 billion gallons, down 6% year-over-year, while first quarter gross profit was $254 million, up 10% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Outlook

  • “For full year 2026, we are updating our adjusted EPS guidance to $2.65 to $2.85 per share, up from the prior range of $2.20 to $2.40 per share.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “The increased guidance is really a reflection of the Q1 overperformance that we have already kind of recorded… we’re just kind of maintaining where we were before for the balance of the year.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “Considering where we informally guided to for the first quarter… we’re just assuming that we generate the same level of profitability over the balance of the year that we expected going in.” (CEO & Director Birns)

  • “Looking to the second quarter, we expect Marine gross profit to be lower sequentially as pricing volatility moderate, though gross profit should be meaningfully higher year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “Heading into the second quarter, we expect our Aviation gross profit to be up sequentially… as well as up year-over-year with the inclusion of Universal Trip Support acquisition.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Financial Results

  • “Volumes were approximately 4 million metric tons in the first quarter, up 4% year-over-year and gross profit was $66 million, up a significant 86% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “For the first quarter, Aviation volume was down 5% as expected. However, gross profit was $138 million, up 20% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “Our Land business delivered results in line with our expectations in the first quarter with volume and gross profit down 15% and 38% year-over-year, respectively, reflecting the impact of our portfolio actions and previously announced business exits.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “Operating expenses in the first quarter were $181 million, up 2% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “Net interest expense in the quarter was $26 million, up versus prior year, driven in part by a reduction in interest income as well as additional working capital requirements during the quarter as prices increased.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “Driven mainly by a sharp increase in commodity prices… our first quarter operating cash flow was negative $46 million and free cash flow was negative $60 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Q&A

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: asked whether the higher full-year guide implied “a pullback” as volatility fades; Executive VP & CFO Tejada said, “Our guidance for the remainder of the year kind of holds consistently,” and CEO & Director Birns added, “we decided to play the same,” while noting “there’s certainly a possibility for some upside.”

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: pressed if volatility had “already back down”; CEO & Director Birns answered, “It’s not back to where it was,” adding, “No one knows how long that’s going to last.”

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: asked about Marine sustainability; CEO & Director Birns said, “Average prices… at the peak doubled in March versus February’s average,” and added, “They backed off about 20% from that max in April, but they’re still well above February’s average.”

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: asked about seasonality and Q3; CEO & Director Birns said, “That’s really more of an Aviation seasonality thing,” and President John Rau warned, “We’ve seen a lot of the airlines announcing schedule reductions.”

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: asked what drove Aviation gross profit per gallon; Executive VP & CFO Tejada said, “Universal Trip Support with the services business, there’s no volume associated with that,” and added there was “some government-related activity” that “is not a massive part of our business.”

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: raised credit extension and receivables; CEO & Director Birns said, “First quarter was literally a hand-to-hand combat customer by customer,” and added, “we monitor it on a day-to-day basis.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • “So great job in a volatile environment” and repeated follow-ups on “pullback,” Marine profitability, airline schedules, and credit suggested slightly negative-to-neutral analyst tone focused on durability and risk. (Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities)

  • Prepared remarks were confident (“we delivered a strong start to 2026”), while Q&A was more cautious on sustainability (“No one knows how long that’s going to last” and “that can go away quickly”). (CEO & Director Birns; Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • Versus last quarter’s repositioning and baseline framing, management language shifted to emphasizing volatility-driven upside already realized and a guidance reset reflecting Q1 (“This reflects our overperformance to date”). (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current-quarter emphasis moved from leadership transition and portfolio reset to execution in volatility and a rebrand announcement (“World Fuel will serve as our unified corporate and commercial brand”). (CEO & Director Birns)

  • Guidance language changed from initiating a full-year adjusted EPS framework and setting $2.20-$2.40 to raising the full-year range to $2.65-$2.85, while reiterating baseline assumptions for the remaining quarters. (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • Marine discussion shifted from expecting “generally in line” first-quarter performance in a “low fuel price and volatility environment” to describing a quarter where “average bunker prices [rose] approximately 70% month-over-month” and Marine delivered “our third best quarter on record.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • Analyst focus stayed concentrated on the same core sensitivities—Marine volatility/optionality and cadence/seasonality—but added sharper attention to balance-sheet mechanics in a price spike (credit extension and working capital). (Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities)

Risks and Concerns

  • “As conditions shifted rapidly following the escalation of the conflict in the Middle East,” management highlighted uncertainty and operational strain from “rapid price changes logistical challenges and tightening conditions.” (CEO & Director Birns)

  • “If the conflict in the Middle East continues for an extended period, it could begin to more broadly impact global supply and customer demand.” (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

  • “We’ve seen a lot of the airlines announcing schedule reductions,” flagged as a possible offset to seasonal aviation strength. (President Rau)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a volatility-driven upside quarter—particularly in Marine—while raising 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to $2.65-$2.85 primarily to reflect profitability already generated and keeping the rest-of-year baseline assumptions intact. The call also introduced a broad rebrand to “World Fuel,” reiterated that Land exits should be “materially complete by the end of the second quarter,” and stressed closer day-to-day credit and working-capital management as fuel prices rose.

data.title: Brandywine outlines $305M asset sales closings in Q2 while planning $100M 7-year financing at ~5.7%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Brandywine said Q1 results tracked plan and left its full-year framework intact: "the first quarter produced results very much in line with our business plan" and "all of our full year operating and financial metrics remain unchanged from our original '26 business plan" (President, CEO & Trustee Jerry Sweeney).
  • On capital recycling and leverage, Sweeney highlighted execution pace and pricing: "Our portfolio recycling and debt reduction program is progressing very much on schedule with approximately $305 million of potential sales under agreement" and "we do expect the majority of these transactions to close in the second quarter" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Sweeney pointed to leasing and market share in Philadelphia: "tenants continued flight to quality" and "41% of all new leases signed in this market was at a Brandywine property" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • On balance sheet actions and optionality, Sweeney reiterated priorities but flagged buybacks: "the clear priority is to lower leverage and return to investment-grade metrics" and "we do anticipate...utilizing a portion of those sales to repurchase our shares" with "about $82 million available under our existing share repurchase program" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Financial framing from the CFO: "Our first quarter net loss was $48.9 million or $0.28 per share" and "Our first quarter FFO totaled $20 million or $0.11 per share" (Executive VP & CFO Thomas E. Wirth).

Outlook

  • Management reiterated the full-year FFO midpoint while tightening the range: "We have narrowed...our full year FFO guidance while maintaining our $0.55 full year midpoint" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Wirth laid out Q2 earnings drivers and key line items: "Property level operating income will total about $72.3 million"; "FFO contribution from our joint ventures will be a negative $900,000"; "G&A expense for the second quarter will total about $9.5 million"; and "Our interest expense...will approximate $43 million" (Executive VP & CFO Wirth).
  • On the capital plan and leverage trajectory, Wirth stated: "Based on the capital plan, we anticipate having approximately $10 million of net outstanding on a line of credit" and "We anticipate net debt to EBITDA will be within the range of 8.4 to 8.8, and our fixed charge coverage will be about 1.8 to 2.0" (Executive VP & CFO Wirth).

Financial Results

  • Wirth attributed the GAAP loss to impairments: "Our net loss was impacted by onetime noncash charges for property impairments totaling about $11.9 million or $0.07 a share" (Executive VP & CFO Wirth).
  • On operating variance, Wirth said: "Property level NOI is $70.2 million was $800,000 above our current reforecast, due to better margins throughout the portfolio" (Executive VP & CFO Wirth).
  • Portfolio and liquidity datapoints included: "Our wholly-owned Core Portfolio is 88.3% occupied and 89.9% leased" and "we remain in solid shape with only $65 million outstanding...and $36 million of cash on hand" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Asset-sale pricing expectations were reiterated: "we are anticipating $290 million of wholly-owned sales at the midpoint" and "those cap rates continue to price at roughly 8% on a cash" basis (Executive VP & CFO Wirth).

Q&A

  • Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked how strong buyer interest affects incremental sales beyond contracts; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said marketing generated "between 7 to 10 qualified bids" and added, "we have a number of other properties...in the market for sale" while closings are "scheduled for the next 60 to 90 days."
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked how Brandywine would balance buybacks vs. deleveraging; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney responded, "the primary objective...is to improve the credit metrics" and buybacks would be on a "leverage neutral, earnings-neutral basis."
  • Manus Ebbecke, Evercore: asked about demand for the planned 902 redevelopment in Austin; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said prospects are "mostly in market" and management is preparing to move "fairly expeditiously" if traction persists.
  • Manus Ebbecke, Evercore: asked for a life science update; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said, "we are seeing the proverbial green shoots" but noted "a macro overhang of regulatory risk" and described 3151 prospects as "slow in their execution pace."
  • Dylan Burzinski, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division: asked mix of core vs. noncore dispositions; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said, "we have one asset that we would consider to be core that we're selling."
  • Dylan Burzinski, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division: asked about confidence in a 7.5% yield on cost at 3151; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said, "we still feel confident about hitting that target" and cited "no real price resistance" alongside longer terms and improved economics.
  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked about the probability/timing of six One Uptown proposals; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said, "our hope is that we get at least half of those across the finish line" and flagged an anchor tenant call right later in the year.
  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked about Solaris/One Uptown recap demand and changes; President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney said Solaris renewals are seeing "about a 16% uptick" and added, "we have no concerns at all about the ability for us to execute on the recap."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly positive, concentrating on execution risk and capital allocation (dispositions depth, buybacks vs. deleveraging, 3151 leasing economics, and Austin redevelopment), with questions framed around timing and probability rather than balance-sheet distress.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, using confidence language such as "we still feel confident" and "we have no concerns at all" while acknowledging pacing issues like prospects being "slow in their execution pace" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management kept a similar emphasis on returning to investment-grade metrics, but Q1 showed more concrete late-stage asset-sale progress ("$305 million...under agreement") rather than earlier-stage disposition positioning ("$100 million with buyers selected" in Q4) (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance posture was consistent, with Q1 reiterating "our $0.55 full year midpoint" and describing a narrowed full-year range; Q4 introduced the initial $0.51 to $0.59 range around that midpoint (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Disposition execution advanced from Q4’s "approximately $100 million with buyers selected" to Q1’s "$305 million...under agreement" with expected Q2 closings (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Liquidity shifted to modest revolver usage: Q4 ended with "no outstanding balance" on the unsecured line and $32 million cash, while Q1 ended with $65 million drawn and $36 million cash (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Austin weakened in occupancy in management’s remarks (Q4: "74% occupancies"; Q1: "Austin is 70% occupied"), while Philadelphia remained highly leased (Q4 combined holdings 95% leased; Q1 combined holdings 95% leased) (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Management lineup changed: Q4 included Executive VP of Operations George D. Johnstone (and announced his retirement), while Q1 did not include him and instead listed Dan Palazzo on the call (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).

Risks and Concerns

  • Austin remained a drag: "Austin is 70% occupied" and "continues to lag the rest of our portfolio" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Joint venture earnings sensitivity to rates was explicit in Q2 guidance: "FFO contribution from our joint ventures will be a negative $900,000...due to higher interest rates on some of the floating rate debt" (Executive VP & CFO Wirth).
  • Execution timing at 3151 was highlighted as the key variable: "The timing of that -- of getting leases exited has been really one of the more challenging aspects" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).
  • Portfolio churn was expected: "Tenant retention was around 45%, very much as expected since we know there'll be a number of known move-outs" (President, CEO & Trustee Sweeney).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1 tracked plan, maintained a $0.55 FFO midpoint while narrowing the full-year range, and shifted investor focus to near-term balance-sheet execution: closing most of $305 million of assets under agreement, using proceeds primarily for debt reduction, and keeping buybacks as an earnings-neutral, leverage-neutral option under an $82 million authorization. Operationally, the company highlighted continued Philadelphia strength and market share gains, while reiterating that Austin occupancy, floating-rate exposure in joint ventures, and the pace of leasing at 3151 remain the key swing factors for improving leverage metrics through 2026.

data.title: VeriSign targets 3.1%-4.3% 2026 domain base growth following a $0.71 .com price increase notice
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: VeriSign (VRSN) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Executive Chairman, CEO & President D. Bidzos said VeriSign delivered “strong results in the first quarter of 2026, both operationally and financially,” with the combined .com and .net domain name base at “a record 176.1 million names,” and “new registrations are the largest we have seen since the first half of 2021, combined with very strong renewal rates.”

  • CEO Bidzos highlighted capital returns and liquidity, saying, “we returned over 100% of our free cash flow to the investing public in the last 12 months for a total of $1.13 billion through share repurchases and dividends,” and added that quarter-end cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities were $556 million, with $863 million remaining under the current repurchase program.

  • CEO Bidzos announced a .com wholesale price change: “we have given notice of a price increase of $0.71 to the annual wholesale price for .com domain names, which raises the wholesale price from $10.26 to $10.97 effective November 1, 2026,” and added, “this is the first allowable price increase since the notice 2 years ago in February 2024 of a $0.67 increase.”

  • Executive VP & CFO John Calys said, “for the quarter ended March 31, 2026, the company generated revenue of $429 million, up 6.6% from the same quarter a year ago,” and added, “this resulted in diluted earnings per share of $2.34 for the first quarter this year.”

Outlook

  • Management raised and narrowed its 2026 domain name base growth outlook: CEO Bidzos said, “we are increasing and narrowing our guidance for domain name base growth to be between 3.1% and 4.3% for 2026,” after citing Q1 trends including “very strong renewal rates” and strength “coming from the U.S. and EMEA.”

  • CFO Calys updated full-year 2026 financial guidance, saying, “revenue is now expected to be between $1.730 billion and $1.745 billion,” and “operating income is now expected to be between $1.170 billion and $1.185 billion,” while interest expense and nonoperating income net “is still expected to be an expense of between $57 million and $67 million,” and capital expenditures “are still expected to be between $55 million and $65 million.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, the company’s domain base growth range moved up (previously 1.5% to 3.5%), and financial guidance ranges were adjusted upward (previously revenue of $1.715 billion to $1.735 billion; operating income of $1.160 billion to $1.180 billion).

Financial results

  • CFO Calys reported Q1 operating income of $294 million and net income of $215 million, and noted operating expense of $135 million “compared to $140 million last quarter,” adding, “as noted last quarter, Q4 2025 results included an impairment charge.”

  • CFO Calys reported cash generation and year-over-year free cash flow movement: “operating cash flow for the first quarter of 2026 was $272 million,” and “free cash flow was $265 million compared with $291 million and $286 million, respectively, in the year ago quarter.”

  • CEO Bidzos stated the board approved a quarterly dividend: “approved a cash dividend of $0.81 per share… to stockholders of record as of the close of business on May 19, 2026, payable on May 27, 2026,” and said VeriSign “intends to continue to pay a cash dividend on a quarterly basis, subject to market conditions and approval.”

Q&A

  • Robert Oliver, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about the mix of AI tailwinds vs. marketing program impact on domain strength; Executive Chairman, CEO & President Bidzos answered, “it’s difficult to really separate the 2… I believe that engagement with our programs, which we know for a fact is a significant contributor, but to what extent is difficult because that demand and our programs sort of collide.”

  • Robert Oliver, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about renewal cohorts after marketing changes; Executive VP & CFO Calys replied, “our renewal rate at 76.3% was very strong,” and added, “the strength of new registrations in the second half of 2025 will present a little bit of a challenge this year because we’ll have a higher proportion of first-time renewing names through the second half of 2026.”

  • Robert Oliver, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about the upcoming ICANN new gTLD program; Executive Chairman, CEO & President Bidzos said, “we’re taking the necessary technical steps to be ready should we choose to be an applicant,” and added, “the new generic TLDs… are likely not launched until 2028.”

  • Jamesmichael Sherman-Lewis, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked about renewal impacts after the .com price increase and about .net pricing; Executive VP & CFO Calys said, “it’s very dependent on what our retail registrars do pricing-wise,” while Executive Chairman, CEO & President Bidzos added, “we have not, at this point today, announced a price increase for .net.”

  • Jamesmichael Sherman-Lewis, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked whether AI-driven traffic requires incremental infrastructure investment; Executive Chairman, CEO & President Bidzos said, “we have multiple orders of magnitude in excess capacity as one component of our resiliency planning and execution.”

  • Alexei Gogolev, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked for more color on “enhanced security components” and new services; Executive Chairman, CEO & President Bidzos responded, “we believe that there are additional security tools that would be synergistic with the type of high assurance infrastructure that we have,” and said a “series of blogs” will begin “starting as soon as next month.”

  • Alexei Gogolev, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about registrar promotional intensity vs. sustainable demand; Executive Chairman, CEO & President Bidzos emphasized channel complexity and constraints, saying, “we have to be careful to treat registrars equally fairly,” and described an iterative program approach: “listen… assess, adapt, revise.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly positive analyst tone, with questions focused on durability of demand (AI vs. marketing), price elasticity from the .com price increase, registrar promotions, and uncertainty around ICANN’s next gTLD round and potential new VeriSign services.

  • Positive management tone in prepared remarks and steady, explanatory tone in Q&A; CEO Bidzos framed operating momentum and guidance changes confidently, saying, “we’re pleased to report,” and described demand as “sustained,” while responses to pricing and renewals emphasized uncertainty tied to registrar retail behavior.

  • Versus last quarter, management moved from outlining possibilities (pricing optionality and “candidates for new services”) to specific actions and tighter targets (a formal .com price increase notice and increased/narrowed 2026 domain base growth guidance).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance shifted upward: domain base growth outlook moved to 3.1% to 4.3% from 1.5% to 3.5%, while CFO Calys raised 2026 revenue and operating income ranges relative to the prior call.

  • Strategy messaging stayed consistent on marketing programs and AI as demand tailwinds, but the current call added a concrete pricing step for .com and more specificity on ICANN timing (“not launched until 2028”) and the application window.

  • Analyst focus was similar across both calls (AI, marketing effectiveness, ICANN/new gTLDs, and new services), with the current quarter adding more direct probing on price elasticity and .net pricing intentions.

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged renewal-rate mix risk: CFO Calys said higher new registrations in late 2025 mean “a higher proportion of first-time renewing names through the second half of 2026.”

  • Management pointed to channel-driven uncertainty around the .com wholesale price increase, with CFO Calys stating outcomes are “very dependent on what our retail registrars do pricing-wise.”

  • Management characterized ICANN’s new gTLD round as a lengthy and uncertain process, with CEO Bidzos citing “a lot of steps” and potential auctions for contention sets, while saying VeriSign is “still evaluating our participation.”

Final takeaway

VeriSign’s management described record .com/.net domain base levels, strong new registrations and renewal rates, and raised and narrowed 2026 domain base growth guidance to 3.1% to 4.3%. The company also issued formal notice of a $0.71 .com wholesale price increase effective November 1, 2026, while maintaining that renewal and registration impacts will depend on registrar retail pricing behavior. Financial guidance ranges for 2026 were lifted, and management reiterated ongoing evaluation of new security-oriented services and potential participation in ICANN’s next gTLD application round, which it expects to extend into a 2028 launch timeframe.

data.title: Crude oil gains for the fourth straight day as U.S.-Iran tensions escalate further
data.description:
Crude oil futures climbed for a fourth straight session Thursday, with volatility ramping up as hopes fade for a negotiated solution to the U.S.-Iran war anytime soon.

Iran's president and parliamentary speaker issued statements declaring unity behind the country's supreme leader, suggesting a hard line toward any talks, and the Iran Revolutionary Guard flaunted its grip over ⁠the Strait of Hormuz with video of its commandos storming a huge cargo ship.

President Donald Trump said in a social media post that he had ordered the U.S. Navy to "​shoot and kill any boat" caught laying mines in the strait.

"The tensions are tightening a bit more now — the market is pricing in a more intense standoff, with a longer duration," BOK Financial senior VP for trading Dennis Kissler said in a note. "The market is already in a nervous state, and any kind of further escalation is going to be a positive to crude prices, at least in the near term."

"The ceasefire could be unraveling," Mona Yacoubian, director of the Middle East Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, told Bloomberg. "Ships are being boarded. The Iranians are thumbing their nose at the U.S., which has expanded the zone of activity well beyond waters in the Middle East. The naval activity is a concrete escalation."

The market shrugged off news that Libya pumped 1.43M bbl/day earlier this month—the highest level in more than a decade—and an extra 1M bbl/day more than it pumped in March, according to Libya's National Oil Corp.

After hours, Trump said the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by three weeks.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) and Brent crude for June delivery each jumped 3.1% to $95.85/bbl and $105.07/bbl, respectively, the fourth consecutive daily gain for both benchmarks; WTI and Brent crude rose 14.3% and 16.2%, respectively, during the winning streak.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) fell, canceling most of the gains made in the previous six sessions as the Energy Information Administration reported the first triple-digit storage injection of the season; the front-month Nymex May contract settled down 4% at $2.614/MMBtu.

data.title: SkyWest anticipates 2026 GAAP EPS in the $11 area while preparing to launch the CRJ450 with United this fall
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SkyWest (SKYW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Today, SkyWest reported net income of $102 million or $2.50 per diluted share for the first quarter of 2026" (CEO, President & Director Russell A. Childs), adding that results "reflect increased production and fleet utilization" and that SkyWest "still anticipate 2026 will be more profitable than 2025" despite "uncertainty impacting fuel costs and production."

  • "We are also excited to share a prototype of the new CRJ450 product, a reimagined premium 41-seat CRJ200" (CEO, President & Director Childs), describing "first-class overhead bins large enough for all rollaboard luggage, and Starlink WiFi" and saying, "SkyWatch is very excited to launch this new product for United this fall."

  • "Total Q1 revenue of $1.01 billion is down slightly from $1.02 billion in Q4 2025 and up 7% from $948 million in Q1 2025" (Chief Financial Officer Robert Simmons).

  • "We also plan to retrofit our prorate fleet and anticipate that our total CRJ450 fleet will reach approximately 100 aircraft" (Chief Commercial Officer Wade Steel), while also noting, "As of March 31, 29 CRJ550s were in service, and we expect the remaining 21 to enter service this year."

Outlook

  • "For 2026, we now expect to see block hour production slightly lower this summer than we modeled last quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), adding, "We continue to work with our partners on production schedules over the rest of 2026."

  • "We also anticipate our GAAP EPS for 2026 will be in the $11 area, slightly down from the color we gave last quarter, reflecting our expectation of ongoing elevated fuel costs" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons).

  • "We are exposed to fuel costs only on roughly 10% of our flying or 40 million gallons needed in our prorate business over the remainder of the year" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), and he added, "We also believe, however, that higher fuel costs will come with some favorable prorate pricing offsets."

  • "To keep it simple, on a GAAP EPS basis, we anticipate directionally that Q2 could be up slightly from Q1 GAAP results of $2.50" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), followed by "Q3... could be up over Q2" and "Q4 could be down modestly from Q3."

Financial Results

  • "Q1 pretax income was $108 million" and "our effective tax rate was 6%" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), who said GAAP EPS "included a $0.29 impact from this unusually low effective tax rate from a discrete benefit in the quarter."

  • "Q1 revenue includes contract revenue of $810 million" and "prorate and charter revenue was $168 million" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons). He also said "leasing and other revenue was $35 million," and attributed the sequential decline to "discrete maintenance services provided to third parties in Q4 that was not expected to repeat in Q1."

  • "As of the end of Q1, we have $241 million of cumulative deferred revenue that will be recognized in future periods" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons).

  • "We ended the quarter with cash of $627 million" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), and he detailed Q1 capital deployment including "repaying $116 million in debt," "investing $102 million in CapEx," and "buying back 783,000 shares of SkyWest stock in Q1 for $75 million" with "$138 million remaining" under authorization.

Q&A

  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "Do you think those mainline carrier cuts are now fully reflected in your schedule?" Wade Steel: "we do expect our block hours to be slightly less than what we talked about last quarter" and "we have pretty good visibility to what's going to happen over the next quarter."

  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: "what the conversion time around that is" for CRJ450; Wade Steel: "The transition time will be a couple of weeks" and "all of the economics are included in the rates with our major partner."

  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division: asked about Chicago underserved-community service and FAA actions; Russell A. Childs: "nothing has necessarily changed in our intent" and "the bids could go to one of our other hubs."

  • Thomas Fitzgerald, TD Cowen, Research Division: asked about unit costs if schedules change; Wade Steel: "We have some levers on training, on hiring" and "a lot of our maintenance costs are variable."

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities: asked if pilot hiring and growth investment change with lower utilization; Russell A. Childs: "we're proceeding full steam ahead" and "The investment that you were planning to make this year... has not changed."

  • John Godyn, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked about essential air service budget risk; Russell A. Childs: "we see this come up quite a bit" and said SkyWest serves small communities "like we've done for 53 years," while Wade Steel said, "We serve currently about 40 different communities."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to cautious on partner capacity cuts, utilization, and policy risk, including questions referencing "capacity cut announcements" (Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs) and "jitters" around essential air service (John Godyn, Citigroup).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive but more risk-aware, emphasizing stability while acknowledging change, including "we do expect our block hours to be slightly less" (Chief Commercial Officer Steel) and "we continue to monitor the situation very carefully" (CEO, President & Director Childs).

  • Versus last quarter’s more upbeat tone on utilization and EPS color increases, this call introduced softer summer production language and framed the EPS change as fuel-driven, with "slightly down from the color we gave last quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q4’s more constructive production outlook ("mid-single-digit percentage growth in block hours" for 2026) to Q1’s "block hour production slightly lower this summer than we modeled last quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons).

  • Earnings color moved from Q4’s "mid-$11" EPS framing to Q1’s "GAAP EPS for 2026 will be in the $11 area, slightly down" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), with Q1 explicitly tying the adjustment to "ongoing elevated fuel costs" and providing the "40 million gallons" exposure framework.

  • Strategic emphasis expanded from contract extensions and parked-aircraft redeployment to a more product-specific narrative around the CRJ450 launch, with United service "begin this fall" and a target of "approximately 100 aircraft" (Chief Commercial Officer Steel).

Risks and Concerns

  • "We expect our block hours to be slightly less than what we talked about last quarter" (Chief Commercial Officer Steel), and he cited drivers including "a little bit in Chicago" and "cleanups with utilization" in CPAs.

  • "We've continued to face challenges in our third-party MRO network, including labor and part shortages" (Chief Commercial Officer Steel), while maintaining that "maintenance expense in 2026" is expected to "remain consistent with 2025" as aircraft return from storage.

  • On fuel, management flagged that "the future cost of fuel is obviously uncertain" (Chief Financial Officer Simmons), while positioning contractual insulation (fuel exposure on "roughly 10%" of flying).

Final Takeaway

SkyWest management positioned Q1 profitability and balance-sheet actions alongside a more cautious 2026 setup: slightly lower summer block hours than previously modeled and GAAP EPS now described as "in the $11 area" due to fuel. The company highlighted fleet initiatives as near-term catalysts—especially the CRJ450 launch with United this fall and continued CRJ550 inductions—while acknowledging operational constraints from MRO capacity and schedule variability at major partners.

data.title: Coursera reaffirms $805M-$815M 2026 revenue outlook while targeting ~$115M run-rate cost synergies within 24 months of Udemy close
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Coursera (COUR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Coursera delivered a strong start to the year" (CEO, President & Director Gregory Hart), highlighting Q1 revenue of $196 million (+9% year-over-year), 7.6 million new registered learners, and "more than 200 million" cumulative learners.
  • "Our planned combination with Udemy will further strengthen our ability to meet this moment" (CEO Hart), positioning the deal as a scale and product-velocity accelerant, including workforce insights from "Udemy's 17,000 enterprise customers" and Coursera’s milestone of "205 million cumulative registered learners."
  • "Our platform now offers more than 1,300 AI courses" (CEO Hart), with engagement of "more than 20 enrollments per minute in Q1," alongside new partner activity such as "Google launched its first AI professional certificate on Coursera" and an expansion with Microsoft via "11 new professional certificates."
  • "Coursera entered the year in a position of financial strength" (CFO, Senior VP, Treasurer & Principal Accounting Officer Michael Foley), citing Q1 non-GAAP gross margin of 57% and describing drivers including "early benefits from the platform fee introduced at the start of the year" and "higher engagement with Coursera-produced content."

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter, we expect revenue to be in the range of $196 million to $200 million" and "We expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $12 million to $16 million" (CFO Foley).
  • "For full year 2026, we are reaffirming our prior guidance" (CFO Foley), guiding to revenue of $805 million to $815 million and adjusted EBITDA of $70 million to $76 million, while stating "today's guidance reflects Coursera's expectations on a stand-alone basis and does not include any impact from the proposed Udemy transaction."
  • Compared with the prior call’s framing that the platform fee would drive "some initial expansion in Consumer segment margins in the second half of 2026" and that Enterprise margin improvement would follow "in 2027" (CFO Foley, Q4 2025), Q1 emphasized that the platform fee is already "beginning to impact consumer margin and will gradually ramp over time" (CFO Foley).

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q1 revenue of $196 million, non-GAAP gross profit of $111 million, non-GAAP gross margin of 57%, net income of $12 million (6.3% of revenue), and adjusted EBITDA of nearly $14 million (6.9% of revenue).
  • The company reported Q1 free cash flow of $3 million, including "approximately $6 million in purchases of content assets" and "more than $11 million of cash payments related to onetime M&A transaction costs" (CFO Foley).
  • The company reported Consumer revenue of $130 million (+10% year-over-year) and Enterprise revenue of $66 million (+7% year-over-year), with Enterprise net retention rate of 90% and paid enterprise customers up 5% year-over-year.
  • The company reported cash and cash equivalents of approximately $790 million and "no debt" (CFO Foley), and said it expects "the anticipated announcement of a sizable share repurchase program in short order following the close of the proposed transaction with Udemy" (CFO Foley).

Q&A

  • Bryan Smilek, JPMorgan: asked about making the product "more actionable" and balancing Consumer strength with Enterprise integrations; CEO Hart said Coursera is building "an AI-enabled search experience" and pushing experiences like "Role Play and voice-enabled Role Play," while noting that for Enterprise, "The Udemy platform on the enterprise side, frankly, is ahead of our Coursera platform" and calling Udemy’s Altus "a core part of our vision for the future enterprise platform."
  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: asked about AI investments for retention and long-term economics; CEO Hart said Coursera is focused on "expanding everything we do around skill assessment and skill verification" and evolving Coursera’s AI tutor to be "less of a reactive one and more of a proactive one," adding that the company believes this can "lead to more engagement and therefore, more retention."
  • Stephen Sheldon, William Blair: asked about flow-of-work learning and gross margin trajectory; CEO Hart said "enterprises are at very different states of readiness," while CFO Foley said the platform-fee impact should "continue to increase quarter-on-quarter and into 2027" and "you should expect continued improvement" in Consumer gross margin.
  • Rishi Jaluria, RBC: pressed on how Coursera is capitalizing on AI-driven labor disruption and on confidence behind the full-year guide; CEO Hart said targeting layoff cohorts is "very hard" but the company is "increasing awareness of Coursera" and seeing strong growth "outside of the U.S."; CFO Foley said caution sits in Enterprise, citing "consolidating tech stacks" and "L&D budgets being under pressure."
  • Josh Baer, Morgan Stanley: asked whether the Udemy deal is affecting Enterprise performance and patterns behind the 90% NRR; CFO Foley said "we're not seeing customers hesitate or wait" due to the merger and described pressure in "larger enterprise where they're downscaling" commitments.
  • Ryan MacDonald, Needham: asked whether AI content benefits Coursera Plus and whether pipeline is inhibited pre-close; CEO Hart said Coursera Plus is "more than 50% of our consumer courses and subscription revenue" and "That percentage is increasing," while adding the company expects the Udemy transaction to close "by the end of Q2" pending regulatory approval in "one country." CFO Foley added that combined enterprise ARR has "about 20% overlap" and said revenue dissynergies are "factored" into the $115 million synergy target.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, with direct pressure on visibility and Enterprise risk, including: "guiding a little bit light of expectations" and "why should we not be worried that maybe that -- there might be another shoe to drop" (Rishi Jaluria, RBC).
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and product momentum, including "strong start to the year" (CEO Hart) and "position of financial strength" (CFO Foley), but more cautious in Q&A on Enterprise: "We're more cautious in our guide for Q2" (CFO Foley).
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 had more pointed Enterprise questioning (macro pressure, retention, and pipeline) while management remained consistent on the Udemy rationale; Q4 highlighted "record free cash flow of $78 million" and a broader confidence arc into 2026, while Q1 repeatedly framed Enterprise as "mixed" and "below our long-term expectations" (CFO Foley).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 kept the stand-alone 2026 framework intact, stating "we are reaffirming our full year 2026 outlook" (CEO Hart), consistent with Q4’s initial 2026 guidance ranges.
  • Q1 sharpened the Enterprise narrative from Q4’s "growth in the low single digits" expectation (CFO Foley, Q4 2025) to a more cautious emphasis on "slower trajectory in Enterprise" for Q2 (CFO Foley, Q1 2026) and ongoing C4B pressure.
  • The platform fee discussion moved from Q4’s "expected to be gradual" and weighted to later periods (CFO Foley, Q4 2025) to Q1’s claim of "early benefits" already visible in Consumer margin (CFO Foley).

Risks and Concerns

  • "In Enterprise, the environment remains mixed, particularly within Coursera for Business" (CFO Foley), with Q&A citing "consolidating tech stacks" and "L&D budgets being under pressure" (CFO Foley).
  • Deal timing and execution risk remained explicit: the Udemy close is "subject to regulatory approval" (CFO Foley), and the company expects Q2 free cash flow to be impacted by "approximately $13 million of transaction-related cash payments" (CFO Foley).
  • Integration risk was acknowledged: "we're being deliberate in managing potential revenue dissynergies" (CFO Foley), and management cited enterprise ARR overlap of "about 20%" across the combined customer base (CFO Foley).

Final Takeaway

Coursera’s Q1 centered on sustained Consumer momentum, improving platform economics tied in part to an early platform-fee benefit, and a more cautious Enterprise posture amid pressured budgets and tech-stack consolidation. Management reaffirmed its stand-alone 2026 outlook and reiterated high-confidence Udemy integration targets, including $115 million of annual run-rate cost synergies within 24 months, while also flagging near-term transaction-related cash impacts and the need to manage potential revenue dissynergies as the deal approaches closing.

data.title: Boyd outlines $650M-$700M 2026 capex plan while reiterating $30M-$35M online EBITDAR guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Boyd Gaming (BYD) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Keith Smith said Q1 results reflected “the benefits of our diversified business, our continued focus on operating efficiencies and our ongoing capital investment program,” highlighting company-wide revenues “nearly $1 billion” and EBITDAR of “$317 million,” with property margins “again exceeding 39%.”

  • CEO Smith described regional strength and Las Vegas headwinds, saying Midwest & South delivered “broad-based revenue and EBITDAR growth” (revenues up 4%, EBITDAR up 5%, margins “nearly 37%”), while Las Vegas Locals faced “continued softness in destination business” (largest impact at the Orleans) and “more significant construction disruption at the Suncoast,” with disruption expected to last “until we complete our renovation project late in the third quarter.”

  • CEO Smith pointed to new openings and pipeline projects, including “Cadence Crossing Casino on March 25,” a Suncoast modernization “on track for completion towards the end of the third quarter,” and a planned Orleans modernization project beginning in 2027. He also said the $750 million Virginia resort “remains on track for a late 2027 opening,” and that Boyd received “final approval from the Illinois Gaming Board” for Par-A-Dice, with the project expected to be completed “late 2028.”

  • CEO Smith emphasized shareholder returns, stating: “We returned nearly $170 million to our shareholders during the first quarter, $155 million in share repurchases and $14 million in dividends,” and added, “we intend to continue repurchases at a $150 million per quarter pace supplemented by our quarterly dividend.”

  • CFO Josh Hirsberg said: “During the quarter, we invested $155 million and expect to spend $650 million to $700 million in capital expenditures for the full year,” including “$300 million related to our Virginia project.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated online and managed segment full-year expectations, with CEO Smith saying, “we reiterate our previous guidance of $30 million to $35 million in EBITDAR for the Online segment this year,” and adding, “we continue to expect our Managed & Other business to generate $110 million to $114 million in EBITDAR for the full year.”

  • Management framed Las Vegas Locals pressure as driven by destination trends and Suncoast disruption timing. CFO Hirsberg said the destination headwind has been “about $5 million, $6 million of EBITDAR each quarter” since Q3 last year and added, “we’re expecting a similar impact in Q2,” with improvement expected to be “down less bad” later in the year and “maybe in the first half of next year, start to see some overall and growth out of that segment.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management language shifted from expecting Las Vegas Locals to “benefit” from Cadence Crossing and Suncoast completion in 2026 to more explicitly outlining Q2–Q3 pressures from “a full quarter in Q2 and a full quarter in Q3” of Suncoast disruption and a slower ramp to “full stride” at Cadence Crossing.

Financial results

  • CEO Smith reported Q1 company-wide revenues “nearly $1 billion,” EBITDAR of “$317 million,” and said “property margins again exceeding 39%,” attributing results to “broad-based strength in our Midwest and South segment,” partially offset by Las Vegas softness and Suncoast disruption.

  • CFO Hirsberg detailed capital allocation and balance sheet metrics, saying Boyd spent $155 million in capex in Q1 and expects “$650 million to $700 million” for 2026; Boyd repurchased “1.8 million shares at an average price of $83.94 per share,” ended Q1 with “74.8 million shares,” and has “approximately $700 million under our share repurchase authorization,” including “an additional $500 million authorized” earlier in April.

  • CFO Hirsberg said Boyd ended the quarter with “traditional leverage of 1.8x and lease-adjusted leverage of 2.4x,” and noted the company now expects to pay “the remaining $290 million during the second quarter” for tax credits related to the FanDuel transaction.

  • CFO Hirsberg flagged corporate expense as temporarily higher, stating, “there was about $6 million of onetime items,” with the “most prominent” related to “charitable contributions” timing.

Q&A

  • Steven Wieczynski, Stifel: asked when destination softness might inflect amid higher fuel prices; President & CEO Smith said it was “hard to kind of discern the impact of gas prices,” pointing to “easier comparisons” in the second half; CFO Hirsberg added the destination impact has been “about $5 million, $6 million of EBITDAR each quarter” and said recovery would likely be gradual.

  • Barry Jonas, Truist: asked why corporate expense was up and how to think about M&A; CFO Hirsberg cited “about $6 million of onetime items,” and President & CEO Smith said Boyd remains disciplined and would act “if the right opportunity presents itself.”

  • Shaun Kelley, BofA: questioned why Fremont traffic declined versus strip data and why locals lag regionals; President & CEO Smith confirmed “an 11% year-over-year decline” under the Fremont canopy and said he was “not overly concerned,” while adding “our pure locals business…is actually quite good.”

  • Benjamin Chaiken, Mizuho: pressed on bridging second-half Las Vegas moving parts and asked about expanding the Virginia temporary facility; CFO Hirsberg outlined Q3 pressures from destination and Suncoast and a Q4 setup with “Suncoast in the rearview mirror and Cadence hitting full stride,” while President & CEO Smith said there was “no ability to make this any larger” in Norfolk and that the temporary casino “is a breakeven.”

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked about intra-quarter cadence and cash taxes; President & CEO Smith cited weather/calendar dynamics and CFO Hirsberg said Suncoast disruption became most visible in March; on taxes, CFO Hirsberg said he was “currently estimating…about $45 million to $50 million” of cash tax benefit tied to accelerated depreciation timing.

  • James Hardiman, Citi: asked to quantify Suncoast disruption and whether Locals can grow; CFO Hirsberg said Las Vegas Locals EBITDAR was down “about $6.5 million,” attributing “probably $5 million to destination” and “about $1.5 million to Suncoast disruption,” and outlined Q2 and Q3 Suncoast impacts of “$2.5 million to $3 million” and “$2 million to $2.5 million,” respectively.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, repeatedly pressing on Las Vegas destination softness, construction disruption, and the likelihood of Las Vegas Locals growth, with framing such as “pretty significant headwind” (Steven Wieczynski, Stifel) and “hold out hope” concerns (James Hardiman, Citi).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing uncertainty and timing; CFO Hirsberg said, “I don’t think we expect it to flip on a dime,” while President & CEO Smith used qualifiers such as “hard to tell” on the destination inflection.

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s posture moved from confidence that Suncoast disruption might be hard to discern to acknowledging, “we’re seeing it, and we’re letting you know” (CFO Hirsberg), while analysts stayed focused on the same central issue—destination softness centered on the Orleans.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Current quarter emphasized quantification and timing of Las Vegas pressures (destination impact and Suncoast disruption), while the prior quarter emphasized anticipated 2026 “benefit” drivers (Cadence opening, Suncoast completion, and tax legislation impacts) with less specificity on disruption magnitude.

  • In Q1, management added more concrete capital-return detail (repurchases “at a $150 million per quarter pace,” authorization “approximately $700 million,” and Q1 average repurchase price of $83.94) versus Q4’s emphasis on the ongoing pace without the updated authorization figure.

  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on Las Vegas destination softness, M&A posture, and iGaming optionality; Q1 questions leaned more into modeling mechanics for Q2–Q4 and quantifying specific drags (Suncoast and destination).

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited Las Vegas risks centered on “continued softness in destination business,” construction disruption that “will continue until…late in the third quarter,” and Downtown weakness tied to “an 11% year-over-year decline in pedestrian traffic” on the Fremont Street Experience.

  • CFO Hirsberg highlighted near-term cash uses and timing items, including the remaining “$290 million” tax credit payment expected in Q2 and corporate expense variability due to “onetime items.”

Final takeaway

Boyd management framed Q1 as a diversified, efficiency-driven quarter with Midwest & South growth offsetting Las Vegas destination softness and Suncoast disruption, while reaffirming key 2026 EBITDAR targets for Online ($30 million to $35 million) and Managed & Other ($110 million to $114 million). Management also highlighted a heavy 2026 investment plan of $650 million to $700 million in capex, continued share repurchases targeted at $150 million per quarter, and a clearer timeline for Las Vegas normalization tied to Suncoast completion in Q3 and a gradual easing of destination headwinds.
